title: NEGOTIATING WITH NEHRUVIAN SECULARISM AND HISTORY: A READING OF THE SELECT NOVELS OF SALMAN RUSHDIE, VIKRAM SETH AND SHASHI THAROOR
author: SK MUSTAFA MD N EHSANUL HOQUE

key_claims: Identifies the work as a PhD thesis submitted to the Department of English at Aligarh Muslim University in 2022
States the thesis was conducted under the supervision of Prof. Mohammad Asim Siddiqui
chapters.limitations: Contains only formal identifying information and no scholarly arguments

key_claims: Argues that Salman Rushdie, Vikram Seth, and Shashi Tharoor explore the theme of Nehruvian secularism and history in their respective novels
Claims that Rushdie’s concept of 'hybridity' is a necessary alternative to homogenizing forces of religious purification
Distinguishes between 'party political secularism' (misuse of secularism for power) and 'constitutional secularism' (the legal ideal)
chapters.limitations: Provides high-level thematic overviews without detailed textual analysis of the primary sources

key_claims: Distinguishes Indian secularism from the Western model by its principle of 'principled intervention' for social justice rather than strict separation
Argues that critics like Madan and Nandy view secularism as a Western imposition that actually fuels religious fundamentalism
Analyzes Nehru’s 'The Discovery of India' as a nationalistic project intended to reclaim history from colonial distortions
chapters.limitations: Focuses on theoretical and historical frameworks rather than literary analysis of the novels

chapters.chapter_title: Chapter II: Reception of Nehru in Indian Literature and Cinema
key_claims: Argues that Nehru's secular outlook was shaped by his liberal family upbringing and exposure to European thought
Claims that 1950s Bollywood portrayed minorities through 'sterile representations' of idealized characters to avoid communal offense
Analyzes how post-1980s cinema adopted 'anti-Pakistani jingoism' as a reflection of rising domestic communal polarization
chapters.limitations: Primarily focuses on broad cultural reception and biographical context rather than the primary novels

chapters.chapter_title: Chapter III: Midnight’s Children: The Idea of A Hybrid India
key_claims: Argues that Rushdie uses the 'bastard child' trope to symbolize the inevitability of cultural hybridity in the Indian nation
Claims the novel functions as a subaltern parody of Nehru’s 'Elite' nationalist narrative in 'The Discovery of India'
Contends that foregrounding forgotten figures like Mian Abdullah exposes the selective nature of nationalist historiography
chapters.limitations: Arguments are specific to the textual mechanics of Rushdie's novel and cannot support claims about other literary styles

chapters.chapter_title: Chapter IV: A Suitable Boy: Confrontation of Secular and Communal Forces
key_claims: Analyzes the political struggle between Nehruvian liberals and conservative Hindu nationalists within the early 1950s Congress Party
Argues that the failed interfaith romance of Lata and Kabir symbolizes the social and psychological barriers of post-Partition India
Highlights the disconnect between the 'enlightened' secularism of the elite and the rigid religious practices of the rural masses
chapters.limitations: Arguments are limited to the early 1950s historical context and cannot be extended to later communal waves

chapters.chapter_title: Chapter V: Communalism, Communal Riot and Communal Politics in Riot
key_claims: Argues that Shashi Tharoor depicts communal riots as politically orchestrated 'mechanisms' rather than spontaneous events
Claims the novel portrays a dystopian democracy where state mechanisms (police/judiciary) are complicit in majoritarian violence
Examines the 'minority complex' as a state of powerlessness imposed on Muslims by demands to constantly prove their patriotism
chapters.limitations: Focuses on the specific communal politics of the late 1980s and early 1990s

key_claims: Concludes that secularism has largely failed to penetrate the 'social and cultural domains' of the Indian masses
Argues that fundamentalists use 'sarva dharma samabhaba' as a strategic bridge to replace secularism with majoritarian rule
Asserts that the three novels collectively underscore the necessity of a 'secular foundation in society' to prevent violence
chapters.limitations: Summarizes previous findings and does not introduce new textual evidence or literary analysis
